title: When I joined 5 years ago things were great, compared to the other big 4s I was familiar with, it was like a heaven. Then things went downhill a few years later, probably the worst big 4 now.
pros: When I joined 5 years ago things were great, compared to the other big 4s I was familiar with, it was like a heaven. Then things went downhill a few years later, probably the worst big 4 now.
cons: Mandatory 3-day office return since the beginning of January 2025 was the worst move the firm could have made, blame the new CEO for all the bureaucratic changes and downfall the firm has had since he took over. Prior to it, there was something to salvage working in a renowned corporate like this. Now there is nothing - a lot of employees have left due to the low pay and annual increase which was only 2% last year (which is regularly criticised in the employee surveys), micromanagement, lack of flexibility. Just the tip of the iceberg - the HR department is constantly understaffed and frequent turnovers do not help, leavers are silenced, the culture has become worse. Managers in HR openly complain about junior staff, does not represent a good culture.

title: 1) good exposure to MNC clients 2) good work life balance compared to other consulting big 4 and companies (however depends on your project) 3) Good starting pay, however increments, benefits and overall bonuses are below average
pros:
1) good exposure to MNC clients
2) good work life balance compared to other consulting big 4 and companies (however depends on your project)
3) Good starting pay, however increments, benefits and overall bonuses are below average
cons:
1) Very mismanaged - from changing business model, priorities to absolutely zero communication amongst leadership, absolutely bad change management when it comes to prevention of taking leave, extension of leave and even bonus outcomes when pushed
2) Feels like a horse race, everyone is only bothered about themselves and the project, no real human connection, Culture has deteriorated over the past 3-5 years and is only getting worse. Even culture amongst teams are equally bad, with functional and non functional teams usually not getting along
3) Leaders are not accountable and very easily can put blame on juniors, some leaders are underqualified for their roles with ;ots of politics up top too
4) Very top heavy, absolutely no room for growth for juniors. Some projects may have over 4-5 directors just for the sake of it or to give utilisation. All decisions are usually made by this folks, so as a junior manager you can only action on what they say
5) Firm has also been facing backlash from clients for performance time to time

title: Most of the associate level people were really nice.
pros: Most of the associate level people were really nice.
cons:
Unfortunately, I had a very bad experience. I joined the firm, expecting a very professional and growth-oriented environment. However, the entire company is designed like a pyramid scheme to be honest. This year all promotions were frozen with no heads-up, while several new partners were announced in the same year. Unbelievably unethical. 

Managers are not very smart. Most of them only get to those positions due to favoritism or simply having worked for a set number of years. While in other high-quality firms, you have to prove your merit through business knowledge and intelligence, as opposed to doing the same Copy + Paste over and over. As a result, lots managers here tend to be significantly limited with respect to intellect, creativity, and intelligence. Just because they have a title and are paid more handsomely compared to other Big4 managers, they tend to be very rude, micro-manage and act immaturely, incapable of giving respectful and constructive feedback to lower-ranked associates. Unfortunately, this is totally normalized and pervasive across all levels and divisions of the firm. One director chastised a manager in front of the entire team, and proceeded to talk behind her back to other members to make it seem that her decision was justified. She was later promoted to become one of the partners of the company.

title: Excellent Brand Recognition & Reputation: Working for PwC, one of the "Big Four" accounting firms, adds significant weight and prestige to your resume. It is a globally recognized brand that opens doors to future opportunities. Exposure to High-Profile Clients: You will have the opportunity to work with a diverse portfolio of large, multinational corporations and leading companies across various industries. This provides invaluable insight into different business processes and complex IT environments. Structured Career Progression: PwC has a well-defined career path. As a manager, you are on a clear track for advancement to Senior Manager and Director/Partner levels, with transparent performance metrics and expectations. Comprehensive Training and Development: The firm invests heavily in its employees' professional growth, offering extensive training programs, certifications, and continuous learning opportunities to keep you updated with the latest technologies and regulations. Strong Networking Opportunities: You will be surrounded by a large network of highly skilled and intelligent professionals, both within your team and among your clients. This is a powerful asset for your long-term career. Competitive Compensation and Benefits: PwC generally offers a competitive salary, bonuses, and a comprehensive benefits package that is in line with top-tier professional services firms. Global Mobility: As a global firm, PwC offers opportunities for international assignments and relocation, which can be a significant advantage if you are interested in gaining international experience.
pros:
Excellent Brand Recognition & Reputation: Working for PwC, one of the "Big Four" accounting firms, adds significant weight and prestige to your resume. It is a globally recognized brand that opens doors to future opportunities.

Exposure to High-Profile Clients: You will have the opportunity to work with a diverse portfolio of large, multinational corporations and leading companies across various industries. This provides invaluable insight into different business processes and complex IT environments.

Structured Career Progression: PwC has a well-defined career path. As a manager, you are on a clear track for advancement to Senior Manager and Director/Partner levels, with transparent performance metrics and expectations.

Comprehensive Training and Development: The firm invests heavily in its employees' professional growth, offering extensive training programs, certifications, and continuous learning opportunities to keep you updated with the latest technologies and regulations.

Strong Networking Opportunities: You will be surrounded by a large network of highly skilled and intelligent professionals, both within your team and among your clients. This is a powerful asset for your long-term career.

Competitive Compensation and Benefits: PwC generally offers a competitive salary, bonuses, and a comprehensive benefits package that is in line with top-tier professional services firms.

Global Mobility: As a global firm, PwC offers opportunities for international assignments and relocation, which can be a significant advantage if you are interested in gaining international experience.
cons:
Demanding Workload & Long Hours: This is one of the most frequently cited drawbacks. The role is known for its high-pressure environment, especially during peak seasons ("busy season"), often requiring long hours and weekend work to meet tight deadlines.

Work-Life Balance Challenges: Due to the demanding nature of the job, maintaining a healthy work-life balance can be extremely challenging. This is a common issue across the Big Four, not just at PwC.

High-Stress Environment: The combination of client deadlines, managing teams, and ensuring audit quality can create a high-stress work environment. The pressure to perform and deliver is constant.

Repetitive Nature of Work: While you work with many clients, the core audit tasks can sometimes become cyclical and repetitive, which may not be stimulating for everyone over the long term.

"Up or Out" Culture: Like many professional services firms, PwC has a performance-driven culture that can sometimes be perceived as "up or out," where there is strong pressure to continuously advance to the next level to remain with the firm.

Bureaucracy and Internal Processes: Being a massive global organization, there can be a significant amount of internal bureaucracy and administrative tasks that come with the managerial role.

Significant Travel Requirements: The role often requires frequent travel to client sites, which can be disruptive to personal life and routines.

title: It has a huge network; you can explore a lot of opportunities inside the system. No one can take advantage of you if you are airtight and document everything you do.
pros:
It has a huge network; you can explore a lot of opportunities inside the system. 
No one can take advantage of you if you are airtight and document everything you do.
cons:
Not many projects, actually, to choose from.
If you try to avail yourself of the promises PwC makes and push the HR to do it, they will put you on the radar and try to cut you loose.
HR is the most deceiving group of people; everyone is so afraid of them for a reason I never understood. Even your partner will leave you out to dry if they come into the picture. 
No one is trust worthy, NEVER trust anyone's word. Document each and everything. Your work, anyone appreciates you, ask them to give them in writing. If your supervisor do not appreciate you than take the appreciation from the client, and by the end of the project, when it is the time for appraisal, put it all in front of your partner and HR if your senior does not promote you. You have to be a lawyer taking yourself as a client if you are working in Big4s. That is how the culture is.
PwC, just like any Big4s initiates a lot of career programs to keep a reputation, but you will realise that all of that is a facade. Once you try to really excel in your career through some of their initiatives, they will put a lot of technical hurdles in your way, the partner would become unhappy, and all your effort and ambitions will go downhill, ultimately wasting your time.
